WSAStartup

Initializes the Xbox Winsock layer for use by the title.

int WSAStartup(
  WORD wVersionRequested,  
  LPWSADATA lpWSAData  
);

Parameters

wVersionRequested
[in] Highest version of Winsock support that the caller can use. The high-order byte specifies the minor version (revision) number; the low-order byte specifies the major version number.
lpWSAData
[out] Pointer to the WSADATA data structure that is to receive details of the Winsock implementation.

Return Values

The WSAStartup function returns zero if successful. Otherwise, it returns one of the error codes listed in the following table. WSAStartup does not call WSASetLastError to set the last error code.

Error code Meaning
WSASYSNOTREADY Indicates that the underlying network subsystem is not ready for network communication.
WSAVERNOTSUPPORTED The version of Winsock support requested is not provided by this particular Winsock implementation.
WSAENOBUFS There was not enough memory available to initialize Winsock.

Remarks

Other than XNetStartup, the WSAStartup function must be the first Winsock function called by the title. It allows the title to specify the version of Winsock required and retrieve details of the specific Winsock implementation. The title can only issue further Winsock functions after successfully calling WSAStartup.

The Xbox Winsock Libraries will indicate support for versions up to version 2.2 of Winsock, including versions 1.0, 1.1, 2.0, 2.1, and 2.2. Note, however, that for all versions, the Xbox Winsock Libraries will function as detailed in this documentation, and not necessarily in accordance with other functional specifications for the specific Winsock version negotiated.

Upon return, details of the Winsock implementation will be in the WSADATA structure pointed to by lpWSAData.

Once a successful call to WSAStartup is made, other Winsock calls can be used as needed. When the title has finished using Winsock, it must call WSACleanup once for every successful call the title made to WSAStartup. The final call to WSACleanup will unload Winsock and deallocate resources that were in use by Winsock.

A title can call WSAStartup more than once if it needs to obtain the WSADATA structure information more than once.

Example

The following code fragment demonstrates how a title that supports only version 2.2 of Winsock would make a WSAStartup call:

WORD wVersionRequested;
WSADATA wsaData;
int err;
 
wVersionRequested = MAKEWORD( 2, 2 );
 
err = WSAStartup( wVersionRequested, &wsaData );
if ( err != 0 )
{
    // initialization failed
    return;
}
 
// Confirm that version 2.2 is supported.
// Note that if the implementation supported versions
// greater than 2.2, it would still return 2.2 in
// wVersion since that is the version we requested.
if ( LOBYTE( wsaData.wVersion ) != 2 ||
     HIBYTE( wsaData.wVersion ) != 2 )
{
    // We only want 2.2 and this implementation doesn't
    // support it.  This will not happen in Xbox Winsock.
    WSACleanup( );
    return; 
}

// successfully negotiated the version, proceed.

Requirements

Header: Declared in winsockx.h, for include use Xtl.h.
Import Library: Use Xnet.lib for titles that only support system link play, XOnline.lib for titles that support online play. Use the secure versions of the library file (Xnets.lib or XOnlines.lib) for final testing.

See Also

Winsock Overview, Winsock Functions | WSACleanup, XNetStartup
